reasoning: The question asks if South Korea's TFR will be higher in 2025 than its 2023 baseline of 0.72. Preliminary data from Statistics Korea released in late February 2025 shows that the 2024 TFR already increased to 0.75, reversing a nearly decade-long decline. More importantly, South Korea experienced a massive 14.9% surge in marriages in 2024—the largest spike since 1970. Because out-of-wedlock births are exceedingly rare in South Korea, marriage is a highly reliable leading indicator for births over the subsequent 1–2 years. Official government projections (from the Presidential Committee on Ageing Society) forecast that the TFR will further grow to 0.79 in 2025, with total births expected to exceed 250,000. While the short-lived martial law attempt in December 2024 could inject political and economic uncertainty that depresses conceptions, this would predominantly impact births in late 2025 (October–December). The births for the first 8–9 months of 2025 are already largely fixed by conceptions occurring in 2024 during the marriage boom. Consequently, 2025 has immense structural momentum to comfortably remain above the 0.72 mark. Given the 14.9% marriage boom and the 2024 preliminary actuals of 0.75, the probability of beating 0.72 in 2025 is very high.
